source_paper_title: Rates of Convergence in the Central Limit Theorem for Markov Chains, with an Application to TD Learning
context:
Section 5 (Conclusions), page 19

Under some conditions, the rate of convergence in the central limit theorem for i.i.d. random vectors is \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (Fang et al. 2019). It would be interesting to see if the \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) rate of convergence in Theorem 1 can be improved to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) under possibly additional conditions than the ones used in this paper.
title: Eliminate the \,\(\log n\)\, factor in the Wasserstein-rate martingale CLT bound
latex:
Given an integer dimension \(d\ge 1\), let \(\{\mathcal F_k\}_{k\ge 0}\) be a filtration and let \(\{m_k\}_{k\ge 1}\subset \mathbb R^d\) be a martingale difference sequence, i.e., \(m_k\) is \(\mathcal F_k\)-measurable and \(\mathbb E[m_k\mid \mathcal F_{k-1}]=0\) for all \(k\ge 1\). Define the normalized partial sum
\[
W_n := \frac{1}{\sqrt n}\sum_{k=1}^n m_k,\qquad n\ge 1.
\]
Assume there exists a symmetric positive definite matrix \(\Sigma_\infty\in\mathbb R^{d\times d}\) such that \(\mathbb E[m_k m_k^\top\mid \mathcal F_{k-1}]\) has a well-defined limit in a suitable sense with asymptotic covariance \(\Sigma_\infty\), and assume sufficient moment and regularity conditions so that a quantitative multivariate martingale central limit theorem in \(1\)-Wasserstein distance holds.

Problem: Find verifiable additional conditions (beyond those used to prove the \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) bound) under which one can prove the optimal rate
\[
d_W\bigl(W_n,\, \Sigma_\infty^{1/2} Z\bigr) \le \frac{C}{\sqrt n}\quad\text{for all }n\ge 1,
\]
where \(Z\sim \mathcal N(0,I_d)\), \(d_W\) denotes the \(1\)-Wasserstein distance on \(\mathbb R^d\), and \(C<\infty\) is a constant independent of \(n\) (but may depend on \(d\) and the distributional parameters of \(\{m_k\}\)).
open_problem_quote: Under some conditions, the rate of convergence in the central limit theorem for i.i.d. random vectors is \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (Fang et al. 2019). It would be interesting to see if the \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) rate of convergence in Theorem 1 can be improved to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) under possibly additional conditions than the ones used in this paper.

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
